skill.name: azure-speech-to-text-rest-py
skill.description: Use Azure Speech to Text REST calls from Python to transcribe short audio files without the Azure Speech SDK.
skill.summary: Python REST examples for Azure Speech to Text short-audio transcription.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto signals, but review shows no malicious intent or prompt injection. The command and weak-crypto hits are false positives from Markdown code fences, tables, and links. The real risk is expected use of Azure credentials, local audio files, and outbound HTTPS requests to Azure Speech services.

medium_findings.title: Expected outbound audio transmission to Azure Speech
medium_findings.description: The examples post local audio data to Azure Speech REST endpoints. This is expected for the skill, but users must understand audio leaves the local machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Python examples clearly make HTTPS POST requests to Azure Speech endpoints with audio data. This is legitimate for transcription, but it is real network data transfer.

medium_findings.title: Azure subscription key handling in sample code
medium_findings.description: The examples read AZURE_SPEECH_KEY from environment variables and place it in request headers. This is normal Azure authentication, but mishandling can expose credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly reads the Azure key from the environment and sends it as an Azure authentication header. The context is legitimate service access, not credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Local audio files are read for upload
medium_findings.description: The examples open local audio paths and stream or upload their contents. This is expected, but users should avoid passing sensitive or unintended files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use explicit file reads before network submission. This is necessary for transcription, but it creates a real local-file handling concern.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline backticks. No Ruby shell backtick execution is present in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences around Bash, Python, or JSON examples. They are documentation markers, not executable Ruby backtick syntax.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines contain front matter, Markdown table text, or a documentation URL. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic operation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no cryptographic algorithm usage. The signals appear to be substring or Markdown parsing artifacts.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance findings are false positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines discuss token use and token caching. They do not enumerate host, user, process, network, or operating system details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is Azure access-token guidance. There is no command or code path that gathers local system information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network plus credential plus file access pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines environment credential reads, local file reads, and outbound requests. This pattern is expected for Azure transcription but deserves user awareness.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous combination exists at the pattern level. Semantic review shows the destination is Azure Speech and the behavior matches the stated purpose.

content.user_title: Transcribe Short Audio with Azure REST
content.value_statement: Short audio transcription often needs a direct REST example without installing the Azure Speech SDK. This skill gives Python patterns for authenticated Azure Speech to Text requests.
actual_capabilities: Shows Python requests examples for Azure Speech short-audio transcription.
Explains required Azure Speech key, region, and endpoint settings.
Covers WAV PCM and OGG OPUS content-type headers.
Demonstrates simple, detailed, chunked, token, and async request styles.
Documents recognition statuses, profanity options, and common language codes.
limitations: Only covers short audio up to 60 seconds.
Does not support real-time streaming or interim results.
Does not cover batch transcription or custom speech models.
Requires a valid Azure Speech resource and network access.

use_cases.title: Prototype short voice transcription
use_cases.description: Build a quick Python proof of concept that sends a short WAV file to Azure Speech REST.

use_cases.title: Add transcription to support tooling
use_cases.description: Convert short customer audio clips into text for triage or review workflows.

use_cases.title: Validate Azure Speech credentials
use_cases.description: Test region, key, endpoint, language, and response settings before building a larger integration.

prompt_templates.title: Basic transcription script
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single short audio file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python example that transcribes a 16 kHz mono WAV file using Azure Speech REST and environment variables.

prompt_templates.title: Detailed response handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Extract confidence and recognition status from Azure results.
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the Azure Speech REST example to request detailed results and summarize status, confidence, and display text.

prompt_templates.title: Production error handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve the Azure Speech REST transcription flow with clear handling for bad requests, unauthorized keys, forbidden access, and failed recognition.

prompt_templates.title: Async service integration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced integration using asynchronous HTTP calls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an async Python transcription helper for short Azure Speech REST requests, including token reuse and safe file validation.

output_examples.input: A request to transcribe audio.wav in en-US with detailed results.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a Python REST workflow that reads Azure credentials, posts the audio, checks status, and returns the recognized text.

output_examples.input: A request to explain why an Azure Speech request returned Unauthorized.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists likely causes such as an invalid key, wrong region, missing header, or expired bearer token.

output_examples.input: A request to choose the correct API for a two-hour meeting recording.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that this REST endpoint is not suitable and recommends Azure Batch Transcription or the Speech SDK.

best_practices: Use short WAV PCM 16 kHz mono audio when possible.
Store Azure keys in environment variables and avoid logging them.
Validate file length, format, language, and region before sending requests.
anti_patterns: Do not use this endpoint for audio longer than 60 seconds.
Do not hardcode Azure Speech keys in source files.
Do not upload sensitive recordings without consent and data handling review.

faq.question: Does this skill require the Azure Speech SDK?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on direct REST calls from Python using HTTP libraries.

faq.question: What audio length does it support?
faq.answer: It targets short audio files up to 60 seconds. Pronunciation assessment is limited to shorter clips.

faq.question: Can it process real-time streaming audio?
faq.answer: No. Use the Azure Speech SDK when you need real-time streaming or interim results.

faq.question: Where should Azure credentials be stored?
faq.answer: Use environment variables such as AZURE_SPEECH_KEY and AZURE_SPEECH_REGION. Do not commit keys to source control.

faq.question: Does the audio leave the local machine?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples upload audio to Azure Speech REST endpoints for transcription.

faq.question: Can it return confidence scores?
faq.answer: Yes. Request the detailed response format to receive alternatives and confidence information.
